name: Coalition Formation Strategy
consequences: Building cross-sector alliances strengthens advocacy credibility but risks dilution of technical priorities through stakeholder negotiations. A well-framed coalition could pressure public-sector buyers to prioritize resilience, but may face pushback from vendors reliant on platform monopolies.
options: Establish a multi-stakeholder advisory board with civil society, academia, and public-sector representatives
Leverage privacy advocates to reframe dependency as a democratic accountability issue
Create a technical working group with platform-agnostic developers to co-design fallback protocols
review: Balances technical credibility against political influence. Weakness: Options underemphasize direct engagement with platform vendors to negotiate interoperability terms.

name: Technical Demonstrator Architecture
consequences: Developing open-standard demonstrators validates platform-neutral approaches but requires careful scope management to avoid appearing as competitive product development. Success could shift perception of feasibility, while failure risks undermining credibility with public-sector stakeholders.
options: Build FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication using GrapheneOS as a privacy-first reference implementation
Design browser-native fallback authentication through WebID and decentralized identifiers
Prototype hardware token integration with open-source firmware to bypass OS-specific trust models
review: Navigates technical proof-of-concept against institutional risk aversion. Weakness: Options lack explicit strategies for aligning demonstrator timelines with procurement cycles.

name: Policy Framing Framework
consequences: Reframing dependency as a resilience risk could shift decision-making priorities but requires overcoming entrenched procurement norms. Success depends on translating technical arguments into metrics that resonate with public-sector risk managers.
options: Develop resilience metrics tied to service continuity thresholds for critical public functions
Map supplier concentration risks against EU digital sovereignty regulations and eIDAS2 requirements
Create scenario-based procurement guidelines for platform-neutral identity infrastructure
review: Transforms technical challenges into institutional priorities. Weakness: Options don't address how to counter convenience-based arguments from incumbent vendors.

name: Procurement Influence Mechanism
consequences: Influencing procurement language could institutionalize platform neutrality but faces resistance from existing vendor relationships. Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen is critical but requires careful calibration to avoid perceived interference.
options: Draft mandatory interoperability clauses for identity systems in public-sector RFPs
Initiate dialogue with AltID stakeholders to integrate fallback-path requirements into procurement specifications
Create a certification pathway for platform-neutral authentication solutions aligned with NSIS standards
review: Targets institutional change through procurement levers. Weakness: Options lack strategies for addressing legal constraints on public-sector procurement decisions.

name: EU Standards Alignment Strategy
consequences: Aligning with European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks could amplify domestic impact but requires navigating complex regulatory ecosystems. Success depends on balancing national priorities with pan-EU interoperability requirements.
options: Contribute to European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) working groups on identity resilience
Map platform-neutral requirements to upcoming eIDAS3 modernization proposals
Develop interoperability test cases for cross-border identity verification without platform dependencies
review: Builds regional influence while addressing national needs. Weakness: Options don't specify how to handle conflicting EU member-state interests in identity standards.

name: Interoperability Framework Development
consequences: Establishing open standards for cross-platform authentication reduces vendor lock-in but risks dilution of technical rigor if not tightly governed. This could delay adoption by public-sector stakeholders seeking proven, closed-system reliability.
options: Promote adoption of open authentication protocols through industry partnerships
Develop a certification framework for platform-neutral identity solutions
Engage with EU technical committees to standardize fallback authentication mechanisms
review: Interoperability framework development vs. vendor resistance. Weakness: The options don't address potential legal barriers to open standards adoption.

name: Regulatory Resilience Strategy
consequences: Reframing digital identity as a resilience imperative shifts policy focus from privacy to continuity but may marginalize privacy advocates who perceive this as a trade-off. This could fragment coalition support.
options: Leverage EU Digital Identity Wallet guidelines to mandate fallback access requirements
Draft procurement clauses that explicitly prohibit exclusive reliance on platform integrity APIs
Institute a public-sector audit process for supplier concentration risks in identity systems
review: Resilience framing vs. privacy prioritization. Weakness: The options fail to integrate privacy safeguards into resilience metrics.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Expansion
consequences: Broadening coalition membership increases political pressure on policymakers but risks diluting focus on technical priorities. This could lead to conflicting demands from diverse stakeholder groups.
options: Form a cross-sector task force including fintech firms and civil society organizations
Create a public-education campaign highlighting risks of platform dependency through case studies
Establish a rotating leadership structure to distribute influence among coalition members
review: Coalition breadth vs. technical focus. Weakness: The options lack mechanisms to maintain technical coherence amid expanding membership.

name: Procurement Diversification Initiative
consequences: Introducing alternative procurement criteria pressures vendors to adopt platform-neutral solutions but may face pushback from suppliers with existing contracts. This could slow implementation timelines.
options: Require bidders for public-sector identity contracts to demonstrate platform-neutral capabilities
Create a pilot program for non-traditional identity providers in low-risk service areas
Develop a scoring system that penalizes supplier concentration in procurement evaluations
review: Procurement reform vs. vendor compliance. Weakness: The options don't account for legal constraints on public-sector contracting.

name: International Standards Integration
consequences: Aligning with European identity frameworks strengthens credibility but may require compromises on national priorities. This could create friction with domestic stakeholders resistant to external influence.
options: Contribute technical input to eIDAS2 working groups on fallback authentication
Map Danish requirements to existing EU interoperability standards for digital identity
Establish a bilateral exchange program with EU member states on identity resilience strategies
review: EU alignment vs. national autonomy. Weakness: The options overlook potential conflicts with Danish-specific regulatory requirements.

name: Authentication Protocol Redesign
consequences: Adopting alternative authentication methods could reduce platform dependency but may face resistance from stakeholders invested in existing ecosystems. This shift could accelerate the development of open standards but risks delaying immediate implementation due to certification hurdles.
options: Adopt FIDO2-based fallback mechanisms with hardware token integration
Implement WebAuthn-compatible browser-based authentication for non-mobile platforms
Establish a decentralized identity verification layer using open-source cryptographic protocols
review: Balancing technical innovation with regulatory compliance. Weakness: Options focus on technical solutions without addressing institutional adoption barriers.

name: Regulatory Advocacy Framework
consequences: Pushing for policy changes could institutionalize platform-neutral requirements but might encounter legal or political pushback. This could create long-term structural shifts but requires sustained engagement with fragmented regulatory bodies.
options: Lobby for mandatory platform-neutral authentication requirements in public procurement contracts
Draft regulatory amendments to mandate fallback authentication paths in eIDAS2 compliance
Engage EU technical working groups to prioritize interoperability in digital identity standards
review: Balancing regulatory ambition with political feasibility. Weakness: Options lack mechanisms for incremental policy adoption.

name: Public-Private Collaboration Model
consequences: Forming partnerships with private sector entities could accelerate technical development but may compromise independence. This could create shared infrastructure but risks aligning with commercial interests conflicting with public-sector goals.
options: Establish a cross-sector working group with mobile OS developers to co-design fallback protocols
Create a joint innovation fund with fintech firms for platform-neutral authentication research
Develop a shared testing environment with telecom providers for alternative authentication workflows
review: Balancing stakeholder inclusion with project autonomy. Weakness: Options prioritize collaboration over independent technical validation.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Strategy
consequences: Building coalitions could amplify influence but may dilute focus on technical priorities. This could create broader support but risks fragmentation from competing interests.
options: Launch a targeted awareness campaign for parliamentary digital policy committees
Organize sector-specific workshops with healthcare and tax authorities on authentication resilience
Develop a multi-stakeholder advisory board with civil society and technical experts
review: Balancing broad engagement with focused execution. Weakness: Options lack prioritization criteria for stakeholder involvement.

name: Infrastructure Resilience Planning
consequences: Designing for fallback scenarios could improve continuity but may require significant resource allocation. This could create robust systems but risks diverting attention from immediate implementation needs.
options: Map critical service dependencies to identify non-negotiable authentication pathways
Develop a phased contingency plan for platform integrity failures
Conduct stress tests on alternative authentication workflows under simulated geopolitical disruptions
review: Balancing preparedness with operational efficiency. Weakness: Options focus on hypothetical scenarios without concrete implementation timelines.
